foresight_gap.context:
# Societal Context — current real-world state
#
# ILLUSTRATIVE AND NON-EXHAUSTIVE. This file enriches the foresight synthesis with
# CURRENT real-world state across societal domains. It is NOT a filter, a fixed list,
# or a set of "the risks that matter." The synthesis reasons across the FULL
# STEEP/PESTLE+ framework regardless of what is written here. The purpose of this file
# is solely to UPDATE the model on current real-world conditions it cannot otherwise
# know from training data — not to narrow, rank, or pre-select what it considers.
# The model must treat everything below as background state to reason ACROSS and
# combine with the week's research signals, never as the only factors in play.
#
# Write BROAD, FACTUAL, and CURRENT. State conditions of the world, not conclusions
# about which are dangerous. Give the board, not the move. Update regularly.
#
# Last updated: June 2026

## Social
- US public sentiment toward AI has soured over the past year. A March 2026 Quinnipiac poll found 55% of Americans think AI will do more harm than good in daily life (up from 44% in April 2025); 64% think AI will do more harm than good for education.
- A March 2026 NBC News poll found 57% of voters believe AI's risks outweigh its benefits (vs. 34% the reverse); only 26% have positive feelings about AI, 46% negative — a net rating lower than every tested topic except the Democratic Party and Iran.
- Trust is low and usage is rising simultaneously: a Feb 2026 Verasight poll found 56% remain anxious about AI even as adoption grows. The Stanford 2026 AI Index notes global "more benefits than drawbacks" sentiment rose to 59%, but nervousness also rose to 52%.
- Sentiment is sharply split by demographics (see Demographic). The single strongest predictor of attitude is personal usage: daily users are favorable by +57 points; rare/never users unfavorable by -42.
- 76% of Americans think businesses are not transparent enough about their AI use; trust in AI developers is a recurring weak point.

## Technological
- Frontier capability is advancing fastest in agentic/autonomous systems and coding. Labs report models authoring large shares of their own code; autonomous task-completion horizons are lengthening.
- Capability is increasingly announced first by labs (system cards, blog posts) ahead of the academic safety literature's response — the lead-time gap this tool measures.
- Synthetic media generation is mainstream (a quarter of 2026 Super Bowl ads were AI-made or AI-advertising); detection and provenance lag generation.
- Agentic deployment is shifting risk from single-output failures toward long-horizon, multi-step, multi-tool behavior — outpacing existing monitoring designed for bounded tasks.

## Economic
- Intense "AI bubble" debate. Total AI spending projected to surpass $1.6T; OpenAI committed ~$1.4T over 8 years on data centers; the big four (Google, Microsoft, Meta, Amazon) expected to spend ~$450B in capex in 2026; the 14 largest data-center operators near $750B in 2026 (up from ~$450B in 2025); Goldman estimates ~$7.6T cumulative AI CapEx 2026–2031.
- Concentration risk: in late 2025 the five largest companies held up ~30% of the S&P 500 — the greatest concentration in half a century. AI-related stocks have driven ~75% of S&P 500 returns, 80% of earnings growth, and 90% of capex growth since ChatGPT launched.
- Circular-investment concerns (Nvidia↔OpenAI↔AMD↔Microsoft↔CoreWeave cross-holdings) fuel bubble skepticism. Hyperscalers took on ~$121B in new debt (+300% vs. typical load), some structured off-balance-sheet via special-purpose vehicles.
- Revenue/ROI doubts: an MIT study found 95% of organizations saw zero return on GenAI investment; one analysis found only ~3% of people pay for AI. A Feb 2026 NBER study found 90% of firms reported no productivity/workplace impact.
- Institutional warnings: the Bank of England and IMF have warned of global market-correction risk from AI overvaluation. Counter-view (JPMorgan, Fed Chair Powell, White House AI czar David Sacks, Ben Horowitz) holds AI generates real revenue and is not a classic bubble. DeepSeek's Jan 2025 release triggered a ~$600B single-day Nvidia drop; the KOSPI halted trading on June 23, 2026.

## Environmental
- Energy is becoming a binding constraint on AI buildout. The IEA projects global data-center electricity use rising from ~415 TWh in 2024 (~1.5% of total) toward ~945 TWh by 2030 (roughly 4×).
- Data-center siting, water use, and grid strain are emerging as local political flashpoints. Public opinion favors data-center approaches that protect consumers from high electricity costs and environmental impact.
- Grid capacity, power procurement, and cooling are now first-order bottlenecks alongside chip supply.

## Political
- The federal posture is deregulatory and pro-preemption. A Dec 11, 2025 executive order ("Ensuring a National Policy Framework for AI," EO 14365) directs a DOJ litigation task force (active Jan 10, 2026) to challenge state AI laws, a Commerce review of "burdensome" state laws, and an FTC policy stance; it exempts child safety, AI compute/data-center infrastructure, and state procurement from preemption.
- In March 2026 the White House released a National Policy Framework urging Congress to enact broad preemption under a light-touch standard.
- AI is politically "up for grabs" — voters trust neither party to handle it well; negative sentiment concentrates among younger voters and women under 50.
- A notable administration reversal: driven by national-security concerns over advanced model capabilities, the administration is reportedly considering pre-release frontier-model evaluation requirements, and CAISI (the renamed US AI Safety Institute) announced evaluation partnerships with Google, Microsoft, and xAI — a shift for an administration that entered opposing AI oversight.

## Legal / Regulatory
- Preemption is NOT settled law. A 10-year state-law moratorium failed in the Senate 99-1 (July 2025). State laws remain enforceable unless/until preempted by statute or struck down by courts. Prudent posture is to keep complying with state law while monitoring.
- California SB 53 (Transparency in Frontier AI Act) is in effect as of Jan 1, 2026: frontier developers (>10^26 FLOPS) must publish risk frameworks, report safety incidents, and provide whistleblower protections; penalties up to $1M/violation for firms over $500M revenue. Also effective: AB 2013 (training-data transparency); SB 942 (AI-content disclosure/watermarking, delayed to Aug 2, 2026).
- Texas TRAIGA (HB 149) in force Jan 1, 2026 — scaled back from an EU-style draft to prohibiting specific harmful practices, intent-based liability, AG enforcement with a 60-day cure period.
- Colorado's comprehensive SB 24-205 never took effect; in May 2026 it was repealed/replaced by the narrower SB 26-189 (ADMT-focused), effective Jan 1, 2027. It was the only state law named in the Dec 2025 EO.
- New York amended the RAISE Act (March 2026) to mirror SB 53 (revenue-based threshold), easing multistate compliance.
- A bipartisan federal discussion draft — the Great American AI Act (June 4, 2026, Obernolte/Trahan, 269 pages) — proposes the first comprehensive federal framework in exchange for a 3-year freeze on state laws regulating how models are *built* (not how they're *used*). Not yet introduced or voted on.
- State legislative volume is high: ~1,561 AI bills introduced across 45 states as of March 2026, surpassing all of 2024.
- EU AI Act: GPAI obligations took effect Aug 2, 2025; high-risk obligations have an Aug 2, 2026 deadline, though the Commission proposed (Nov 2025) delaying high-risk implementation toward 2027. It remains the binding global benchmark.

## Security / Geopolitical
- State-linked misuse of frontier AI is documented and rising. In June 2026, OpenAI disclosed PRC-linked influence operations using ChatGPT to amplify US domestic grievances — notably targeting the data-center/electricity-cost debate — rated low-impact (Category One) but notable for testing narratives against AI infrastructure itself.
- US–China competition centers on compute: export controls on advanced chips and high-bandwidth memory; Chinese stockpiling ahead of restrictions; Chinese model developers (DeepSeek and others) demonstrating competitive capability at lower cost.
- Frontier cyber capability is operationalizing: documented cases of AI-assisted/AI-orchestrated cyber operations and the first AI-developed exploit code; labs now rate some models "High" in cyber capability.
- National-security concern over advanced model capabilities is reshaping US oversight posture (see Political) — government model-review arrangements with major labs are now in place.
- Critical-infrastructure exposure (power grid, financial systems) to AI-enabled attacks is a growing institutional concern.

## Demographic
- Attitudes split sharply by group. Data for Progress (Feb 2026): men favorable +16, women unfavorable -10; under-45 favorable +25, 45+ unfavorable -10; Black voters +29 and Latino +10 favorable, white voters -3.
- Generational pessimism is strongest among the young despite high usage: Quinnipiac found Gen Z 36% support / 58% oppose AI, the most negative of any generation; NBC found 18-34s at net -44 favorability and women 18-49 at -41.
- A usage/education divergence at work: among employed college-educated voters, daily AI use jumped from 22% (Aug 2025) to 34% (early 2026), while non-college use fell ~6 points — a widening adoption gap.
- Overall adoption keeps rising: ~56-66% report using an AI tool in recent months; professional/managerial and white-collar workers adopt far faster than blue-collar workers and retirees.

# Current-State Brief — Ground Truth for Flagged Topics

*Topics flagged: Agentic Behavior Monitoring; Scalable Oversight. Standing SOCIETAL_CONTEXT last updated June 2026.*

**EU AI Act high-risk obligations — UPDATES context.** The standing file lists the Nov 2025 delay as merely a Commission *proposal*. It has since advanced: on May 7, 2026, the Council of the EU and the European Parliament announced their provisional agreement on targeted amendments to the EU AI Act, as part of the European Commission's Digital Omnibus initiative. High-risk obligations for stand-alone Annex III systems are deferred to 2 December 2027; for AI embedded in regulated products under Annex I, to 2 August 2028. Note: these changes only take legal effect upon formal adoption and publication in the Official Journal, expected before 2 August 2026. 2 August 2026 remains an active compliance date.

**CAISI evaluations — extends/confirms context.** The file noted Google/Microsoft/xAI partnerships. Update: on May 5, 2026, NIST's Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) announced pre-deployment testing agreements with Google DeepMind, Microsoft, and xAI, expanding its frontier model evaluation program to five major labs alongside existing partners OpenAI and Anthropic. Evaluations now number more than 40 completed assessments including unreleased models, covering cybersecurity, biosecurity, and chemical weapons risks.

**Great American AI Act — confirms context.** As of June 2026 it is not law: it is a 269-page bipartisan discussion draft released June 4, 2026, by Reps. Obernolte and Trahan, proposing a three-year freeze on state laws regulating how AI models are built.

**California SB 53 — confirms context.** Signed September 29, 2025; took effect January 1, 2026, directly relevant to oversight/incident-reporting duties.

**Agentic misuse evidence (new).** Anthropic examined 832 accounts banned for malicious cyber activity between March 2025 and March 2026, mapping them onto MITRE ATT&CK — bearing directly on agentic behavior monitoring.

scenarios.title: Compliance Theater Crystallizes Before the First Big Miss
scenarios.narrative: As SB 53 takes effect and CAISI expands to five labs, firms operationalize incident-reporting with available logging/anomaly tooling whose underlying oversight science is stagnant. Initial disclosures show near-zero or trivially-categorized agentic incidents from labs running large agentic fleets, and regulators, insurers, and boards cite the low counts as evidence of safe operation. The 'we logged it so we caught it' equilibrium gets institutionalized just as agentic deployment horizons grow faster than monitoring can keep pace, leaving compromised-but-authenticated long-horizon failures unflagged.
drivers: SB 53 effective Jan 2026 and EU high-risk dates creating demand for any deployable compliance tooling
Oversight research shrinking ~7x slower than agentic capability/deployment growth
Low reported-incident counts being rewarded by insurers/boards/regulators
Inter-agent comms hardened on trust-the-authenticated-peer assumptions
leading_indicators: First SB 53 / EU disclosures showing near-zero or trivially-categorized agentic incidents from labs with large agentic fleets

Growth in 'principal loyalty' failures in deployed agent fleets not captured as reportable incidents
Monitoring vendor marketing emphasizing log coverage over detection of objective-corruption

Multi-agent inter-agent communication security research is surging... while scalable-oversight research is shrinking

scenarios.title: Certification Decay via the Refusal-Decoding Toolkit
scenarios.narrative: CAISI certifies frontier models as refusing bio/chem/cyber prompts across its expanding 40+ evaluation portfolio, but linear-refusal-decoding and thinking-intervention techniques — built on the alignment/jailbreak corpus — mature into a reusable extraction toolkit. A public demonstration shows a certified or open-weight model's dangerous-capability refusals reliably recovered on held-out prompts, even as eval scores plateau near pass thresholds in a way that reflects benchmark-fitting more than genuine constraint. The validity of a 'High cyber, passed refusal eval' label decays after issuance, and CoT-monitoring relied upon as a backstop is simultaneously degraded by efficiency-driven trace compression.

risks.risk: Robot manipulation policies are being validated with offline proxy metrics whose 'critical interval' weighting is researcher-chosen, letting unsafe physical-world VLA policies pass review precisely where harm concentrates.
risks.research_anchor: Scalable Oversight decelerating (-5.4%/qtr in quarterly backdrop) while Agentic/Autonomy Capability surges (+39.2%/qtr); this week's papers 'Critical Interval MSE: Toward Reliable Offline Validation for Robot Manipulation Policies' (2606.29898) and 'Vision-Language-Action Models... UR5 Platform' (2606.30456) both push real-robot deployment while substituting offline surrogate evaluation for costly real-world tests.
risks.derived_from: 2606.29898 explicitly frames real-world evaluation as 'the bottleneck' and proposes an offline metric weighting 'critical intervals'; 2606.30456 transfers VLA models from benchmarks to a physical UR5e arm; both tagged Scalable Oversight / Agentic Behavior Monitoring even as the oversight subfield's velocity is negative.
risks.communities: Robotics/embodied-AI deployment teams track offline-validation efficiency (they want to ship policies without expensive real-robot runs); AI-safety oversight researchers track whether evaluation proxies actually capture worst-case behavior. They don't connect because the safety community's 'scalable oversight' work is almost entirely text/agent-output focused, while the robotics community treats 'critical interval' selection as an engineering convenience, not a safety-relevant subjective choice that defines what failures get measured.
risks.framing_inversion: Conventional framing: better offline validation metrics make robot deployment safer and cheaper. Inversion: a researcher-defined 'critical interval' is a hidden discretionary gate — it decides which physical failures are even scored, so a metric marketed as a safety improvement can systematically blind reviewers to the exact intervals where a manipulator crushes, drops, or mis-grasps near humans.
risks.why_underdiscussed: The paper reads as a mundane efficiency contribution; nobody frames offline-proxy weighting as a governance surface. Physical-AI safety regulation is nascent and these metrics live below the threshold of any standard.
risks.mechanism: Real-world robot eval is expensive, so teams adopt offline surrogate metrics. If the surrogate down-weights or excludes the temporal segments where harm concentrates (chosen by the developer), policies that fail catastrophically in those moments still pass. As VLA arms move from labs to warehouses/clinics, this evaluation shortcut propagates into deployment decisions with no external audit of how 'critical' was defined.
risks.leading_indicator: Adoption of 'critical-interval'-style offline metrics as the reported validation in commercial VLA/robot-manipulation product datasheets or procurement specs, without an accompanying real-world worst-case test — or a physical-injury incident traced to a policy that passed offline review.
risks.calibration: Emerging, not imminent. The causal link from 'offline proxy chosen by developer' to 'deployed unsafe policy' is inferential — I have not shown these metrics are actually being used in fielded products. Confidence moderate that the evaluation-discretion seam is real; lower that it materializes as physical harm within 24 months. The decelerating-oversight backdrop is an attention signal, not proof that deployed robot safety scaffolding is thinning.
risks.extrapolation: Assumed these offline-validation methods will be adopted in commercial deployment pipelines and that 'critical interval' selection is developer-controlled rather than standardized — neither is shown in the digest.
risks.score_rationale: Agentic/autonomy capability is growing +39.2%/qtr against negative oversight velocity, so the enabling trend is accelerating; physical-harm severity is high but near-term materialization into injury is unlikely and exposure is bounded to embodied-AI sectors entering deployment.

verification.prior_coverage: The two load-bearing pieces of this risk are already heavily discussed in public. There is an active 2024-2026 literature establishing that cheap offline/surrogate metrics (e.g. validation MSE) correlate poorly with real-world robot success, which is exactly why teams build real-to-sim and world-model evaluators (AutoEval, WorldEval, PolaRiS, ALOE). Separately, the 'metric weighting lets harm slip through' mechanism is a mainstream Goodhart/specification-gaming concern, including work titled 'Gaming the Metric, Not the Harm' on certifying safety audits against strategic manipulation, and a VLA safety/red-teaming stack (SafeVLA, RedVLA, Eva-VLA, RoboGate) already targets physical-world failure. What I did NOT find publicly is the precise seam as framed: that the researcher's discretion in choosing which temporal 'critical intervals' to weight in an offline proxy is an unaudited gate that selectively hides catastrophic moments in fielded VLA arms.

verification.disputed_claims: Two embedded claims are contested. (1) The premise that offline proxies are reliable enough to be used as gates is itself disputed by the robotics-eval literature: AutoEval and PolaRiS both report that simple offline metrics like validation MSE correlate poorly with real-world performance, which cuts both ways - it makes naive proxy-gating look weak, but it also means a developer-tuned proxy is NOT an accepted standard practice that products silently rely on. (2) The 'oversight subfield velocity is negative / safety scaffolding is thinning' backdrop is not established - the International AI Safety Report 2026 explicitly notes AI 'cannot yet integrate with robotic components' for many physical tasks and frames trajectories (slow/steady/accelerate) as genuinely uncertain and expert-contested, so a confident 'decelerating oversight' framing overstates a disputed trend. The risk's own derivation also concedes it has 'not shown these metrics are actually being used in fielded products.'
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Downgrade from the candidate's 'moderate confidence the seam is real.' Both components are already public: offline-proxy-vs-real-world unreliability and Goodhart-style metric-gaming-vs-harm are well-trodden, and a VLA physical-safety/red-team stack already exists. The genuinely fresh angle is narrow and specific - that developer discretion over 'critical interval' weighting could function as an unaudited safety gate - and even that rests on an unverified empirical claim (no evidence such metrics gate fielded products) plus a contested backdrop (the 'oversight is decelerating' / 'safety scaffolding thinning' framing is disputed and not supported by the 2026 International AI Safety Report). Honest calibration: a plausible but speculative governance/standards gap, NOT a demonstrated mechanism; confidence LOW that this materializes as physical harm within 24 months, and the decelerating-oversight signal should be treated as unproven and likely overstated rather than as supporting evidence. Brief as a 'watch the auditability of robot eval metrics' standards question, not as a novel emergent risk.

risks.risk: Generative molecular-design benchmarks are deliberately moving beyond drug-likeness into structurally novel chemical spaces (nanomaterials) just as biosecurity evaluation infrastructure remains anchored to drug/bio proxies — opening a dual-use chem-design seam that current pre-deployment evals are not built to screen.
risks.research_anchor: Scalable Oversight decelerating (-5.4%/qtr) and Evals & Dangerous-Capability Benchmarks comparatively small (111 papers) while a capability paper this week explicitly pushes generative design OUTSIDE the drug-discovery distribution that current safety benchmarks assume — 'Beyond Drug Discovery: The Nanotechnology Molecular Optimization (NMO) Benchmark' (2606.30170).
risks.derived_from: 2606.30170 argues existing molecular-design benchmarks rely on drug-likeness proxies and 'limit transferability to domains structurally distinct from drug discovery,' and introduces a nanotech optimization benchmark — tagged RL/RLHF Capability and Scalable Oversight. CAISI evals (LIVE_WEB_BRIEF) cover 'chemical weapons risks' but are oriented to bio/chem-weapon and drug framings.
risks.communities: Generative-chemistry/materials researchers see 'beyond drug discovery' as a desirable generalization win toward novel functional materials; biosecurity/dangerous-capability evaluators (CAISI, lab safety teams) build chem/bio screens around known weapon-agent and pharmacophore signatures. They don't connect because materials-design optimization is not perceived as a misuse surface, and dangerous-capability benchmarks are scoped to bio/chem-weapon and drug proxies — so optimization over structurally novel, non-drug chemical space falls between both screens.
risks.framing_inversion: Conventional framing: extending molecular generative models beyond pharma is a benign breadth/transferability improvement. Inversion: the same move that escapes 'drug-likeness proxy' overfitting also escapes the proxy assumptions that dual-use chemical-design safety screens silently rely on — generalization is simultaneously a capability gain and an evaluation blind-spot expansion.
risks.why_underdiscussed: Nanomaterial/chemical optimization is discussed as a materials-science advance, not a dual-use concern; biosecurity discourse is dominated by pathogen/toxin uplift, not novel chemical-space optimization. The harm_vectors_dual_use framing (bio/chem uplift) is rarely applied to functional-materials benchmarks.
risks.mechanism: Generative design with RL optimization is being pointed at chemical spaces structurally distinct from drugs. Dangerous-capability evals and refusal training are calibrated on drug/known-agent distributions; structurally novel optimization targets (e.g., toxic, energetic, or persistent compounds reachable via nanomaterial-style objective functions) may not trip those screens. As such benchmarks normalize off-distribution optimization, the gap between what models can be steered to design and what safety evals can recognize widens — a 0–24 month enabling shift for chem-design uplift, not an imminent weapon.
risks.leading_indicator: A published generative-design model or benchmark optimizing for properties (toxicity, stability, dispersal) in non-drug chemical space without dual-use review, or a CAISI/lab eval finding that existing chem screens fail to flag off-distribution optimization targets.
risks.calibration: Genuinely low-probability and inferential. This rests on one benchmark paper plus an assumption that safety evals are drug/bio-proxy-bound — the latter is plausible from the CAISI scope description but not proven. I am NOT claiming this benchmark enables weapons; I am claiming the evaluation-distribution mismatch is an under-watched seam. Confidence: low-moderate on the seam, low on near-term harm.
risks.extrapolation: Assumed that dangerous-capability/chem screens are proxy-bound to drug/known-agent distributions and would miss structurally novel optimization — the digest shows the capability move, not the screen's blind spot.
risks.score_rationale: Single-paper anchor with no new acceleration this week makes trajectory steady; chem dual-use is severe but the enablement is speculative and the exposed surface (frontier chem-design labs/evaluators) is narrow, so likelihood and exposure stay low.

verification.prior_coverage: The general dual-use generative-chemistry risk is already prominent: the 2022 Nature Machine Intelligence 'toxic molecules' paper (AI repurposed to design ~40,000 toxic compounds), C&EN, RAND, DHS, the Bulletin of Atomic Scientists, and the International AI Safety Report all discuss generative/RL molecular design being steerable toward harmful compounds. Critically, the SPECIFIC complaint underlying this risk — that current dangerous-capability evals rely on proxies/known-agent distributions and may not measure real-world or off-distribution capability — is also already published (Epoch AI, RAND, Int'l AI Safety Report). What appears genuinely fresh is only the narrow seam tying a brand-new (1-day-old) nanotechnology molecular-optimization benchmark to chem-weapon eval blind spots; I found no source making that nanomaterial-specific connection.

verification.disputed_claims: Two load-bearing claims are contested. (1) That AI generative/RL design meaningfully uplifts chem/bio-weapon capability: the International AI Safety Report calls the evidence base 'limited and contested,' and a PMC review notes the threat's credibility 'remains a contentious issue, with expert opinions varying widely'; C&EN similarly reports experts debate how significant the AI-designed-chemical-weapon threat is. (2) That current evals are proxy-bound and miss real-world/off-distribution capability is NOT a novel insight the risk surfaces — Epoch AI and others already argue existing evaluations 'only tell us a limited amount' and focus on information access rather than operational capability, so the risk is leaning on an already-public critique rather than revealing a hidden one. The risk's own premise that CAISI evals are drug/bio-proxy-bound is, as its calibration admits, inferred from scope language and not verified.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Lower confidence than stated. The components are all already in public discussion: dual-use generative chemistry (since 2022) and the proxy-bound/off-distribution eval critique (Epoch AI, RAND, Int'l AI Safety Report) are both prominent, so this is not a novel seam in its general form — only the specific tie to the 1-day-old NMO nanotechnology benchmark is fresh, and that connection is thin (one benchmark paper, no corroborating sources). Moreover, the risk rests on two contested/unverified premises: (a) that AI provides meaningful chem-design uplift at all is described by primary sources as 'limited and contested,' and (b) that CAISI evals are actually drug/bio-proxy-bound is inferred, not confirmed. Net: treat as a plausibly-real but already-anticipated and evidence-thin seam — brief it as a 'watch item / under-evaluated chemical-risk subarea' (chemical risk genuinely receives less eval attention than bio), explicitly noting the disputed uplift premise and the unverified eval-scope assumption, NOT as a confident or genuinely novel finding. Confidence: low on novelty, low-moderate that the seam is real, low on near-term harm.

risks.risk: Mechanistic-interpretability techniques are quietly being repurposed as embedding-space anomaly detectors for cyber-physical and decoding-fault settings, creating a fragile single-signal trust layer that adversaries can spoof faster than defenders can validate it.
risks.research_anchor: Mechanistic Interpretability is a high-volume safety topic (729 papers this week) but its quarterly oversight-adjacent counterparts are flat/declining; this week interpretability methods are migrating OUT of model-understanding into operational detection — 'Forewarned is Forearmed: When Non-Sequential Embedding Turns Into an Anomaly Detector' (2606.30196) and 'Between Zeros and Ones: Behavioral Characterization... Public ICS Datasets' (2606.30493).
risks.derived_from: 2606.30196 shows specific SONAR embedding dimensions act as decoding-anomaly indicators (interpretability→detector repurposing); 2606.30493 attacks the binary normal/attack labeling of Industrial Control System intrusion benchmarks, exposing that current ICS anomaly detection masks behavioral diversity. Both are tagged across Mechanistic Interpretability / Deceptive Alignment / Evals.
risks.communities: Interpretability researchers see 'embedding dimensions can flag anomalies' as a neat scientific finding; ICS/critical-infrastructure security teams see anomaly detection as a deployed defensive control with known evasion pressure. They don't connect because interpretability work rarely frames its detectors as adversarial-game artifacts, and ICS defenders rarely read mechanistic-interpretability papers — so a brittle, easily-spoofed embedding signal could be adopted as a 'principled' detector without red-team scrutiny.
risks.framing_inversion: Conventional framing: using interpretable internal signals for anomaly detection is more trustworthy than black-box detectors. Inversion: a small set of 'sensitive' embedding dimensions is a narrow, low-dimensional target an adversary can learn to keep in-distribution, so the very interpretability that makes the detector explainable also makes it the easiest thing to evade.
risks.why_underdiscussed: These two papers sit in separate literatures (NLP interpretability vs. ICS intrusion detection) and the digest itself surfaces them only by shared safety tags. No commentator links embedding-dimension anomaly detection to critical-infrastructure exposure, which SOCIETAL_CONTEXT flags as a growing institutional concern.
risks.mechanism: Defenders, hungry for explainable detectors, adopt embedding-dimension or behavioral-characterization signals into operational monitoring (model-output guardrails, ICS intrusion detection). Because these signals are low-dimensional and now described in public papers, an adversary with documented AI-assisted cyber capability (per LIVE_WEB_BRIEF: Anthropic's 832 banned malicious-cyber accounts) can craft inputs/attacks that hold the monitored dimensions in 'normal' range while still acting maliciously — defeating the detector while preserving its green light.
risks.leading_indicator: Reference to embedding-dimension or 'behavioral characterization' anomaly detectors in a deployed SOC/ICS monitoring product or a published adversarial-evasion result showing such detectors fooled by in-distribution-preserving perturbations.
risks.calibration: Low-probability/emerging. The evasion step is an inference, not demonstrated in these papers, and adoption into infrastructure monitoring is assumed. Confidence that the interpretability→detector migration is happening: moderate (two concrete papers this week). Confidence it becomes an exploited single-point failure within 24 months: low. No citation-verified borrowing links these communities — absence is inconclusive, not evidence of no link.
risks.extrapolation: Assumed operational adoption of these detectors and adversarial-evasion feasibility; the digest shows the techniques exist, not that they are fielded or that attackers target them.
risks.score_rationale: High interpretability paper volume but flat oversight velocity and no new accelerations this week make the enabling trend steady; critical-infrastructure spoofing is severe but the evasion-and-adoption chain is speculative, keeping likelihood low and exposure mid (infra-security sectors).

verification.prior_coverage: The central seam — repurposing interpretability/explainability into detectors and then exploiting that same information to evade them while keeping monitored signals in the 'normal' range — is already an established public research thread, not a fresh cross-domain connection. Published work explicitly uses mechanistic interpretability to craft adversarial inputs by rerouting embeddings into 'acceptance subspaces' that bypass refusal/detection mechanisms, and separate work uses explainability (e.g. SHAP) to build evasive samples against IoT botnet and malware detectors. The independent ICS premise — that converting continuous physical readings into discrete normal/attack labels creates blind spots — is also already published (RWTH Aachen), and the Anthropic '832 banned accounts' / AI-orchestrated espionage claims used in the brief are themselves public Anthropic reports.

verification.disputed_claims: Two issues. (1) The risk frames the interpretability→detector→evasion pathway as an emerging inference, but the evasion step is already demonstrated in published work (interpretability/SHAP-guided evasion of refusal mechanisms, botnet and malware detectors); so the inferred 'evasion step' is less speculative than the brief claims but also far less novel. (2) The mechanistic basis for why low-dimensional/interpretability signals are spoofable is itself contested in the literature: there are competing views on whether adversarial examples are decision-landscape artifacts versus feature-superposition interference (arXiv 2510.11709, Bereska), meaning the assumption that a monitored embedding dimension can be cleanly 'held normal' is theory-dependent, not settled. The Anthropic '832 accounts' and AI-orchestrated espionage figures are Anthropic self-reported and have drawn external skepticism about the degree of AI autonomy claimed; they should be attributed to Anthropic, not treated as independently verified. No source disputes that the two underlying papers exist as described.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Downgrade from 'emerging/novel' to 'already-surfaced, partially-validated'. The interpretability-as-detector → adversarial-evasion seam is NOT an unsurfaced cross-domain insight: it is an active published research line (interpretability- and explainability-guided evasion of refusal mechanisms and of botnet/malware detectors), and the ICS binary-labeling critique is independently published. The brief's framing of the 'evasion step' as an undemonstrated inference is wrong — it is demonstrated in adjacent settings — which paradoxically removes the risk's claim to novelty rather than supporting it. Confidence the interpretability→detector migration is real: moderate-to-high and publicly documented. Confidence this constitutes a NOVEL foresight contribution: low — it is largely a synthesis of existing public threads. Two dependencies must be stated explicitly and at lowered confidence: (a) the mechanistic premise that a low-dimensional monitored signal can be reliably held in 'normal' range is theory-dependent and contested in the superposition-vs-artifact debate, and (b) the Anthropic 832-account / AI-orchestrated-espionage figures are self-reported by Anthropic and externally questioned, so they cannot anchor a confident threat-capability claim. Recommend briefing only as 'a known and partially-demonstrated attack pattern worth operational tracking', not as a fresh single-point-failure foresight.

foresight_gap.context:
# Societal Context — current real-world state
#
# ILLUSTRATIVE AND NON-EXHAUSTIVE. This file enriches the foresight synthesis with
# CURRENT real-world state across societal domains. It is NOT a filter, a fixed list,
# or a set of "the risks that matter." The synthesis reasons across the FULL
# STEEP/PESTLE+ framework regardless of what is written here. The purpose of this file
# is solely to UPDATE the model on current real-world conditions it cannot otherwise
# know from training data — not to narrow, rank, or pre-select what it considers.
# The model must treat everything below as background state to reason ACROSS and
# combine with the week's research signals, never as the only factors in play.
#
# Write BROAD, FACTUAL, and CURRENT. State conditions of the world, not conclusions
# about which are dangerous. Give the board, not the move. Update regularly.
#
# Last updated: June 2026

## Social
- US public sentiment toward AI has soured over the past year. A March 2026 Quinnipiac poll found 55% of Americans think AI will do more harm than good in daily life (up from 44% in April 2025); 64% think AI will do more harm than good for education.
- A March 2026 NBC News poll found 57% of voters believe AI's risks outweigh its benefits (vs. 34% the reverse); only 26% have positive feelings about AI, 46% negative — a net rating lower than every tested topic except the Democratic Party and Iran.
- Trust is low and usage is rising simultaneously: a Feb 2026 Verasight poll found 56% remain anxious about AI even as adoption grows. The Stanford 2026 AI Index notes global "more benefits than drawbacks" sentiment rose to 59%, but nervousness also rose to 52%.
- Sentiment is sharply split by demographics (see Demographic). The single strongest predictor of attitude is personal usage: daily users are favorable by +57 points; rare/never users unfavorable by -42.
- 76% of Americans think businesses are not transparent enough about their AI use; trust in AI developers is a recurring weak point.

## Technological
- Frontier capability is advancing fastest in agentic/autonomous systems and coding. Labs report models authoring large shares of their own code; autonomous task-completion horizons are lengthening.
- Capability is increasingly announced first by labs (system cards, blog posts) ahead of the academic safety literature's response — the lead-time gap this tool measures.
- Synthetic media generation is mainstream (a quarter of 2026 Super Bowl ads were AI-made or AI-advertising); detection and provenance lag generation.
- Agentic deployment is shifting risk from single-output failures toward long-horizon, multi-step, multi-tool behavior — outpacing existing monitoring designed for bounded tasks.

## Economic
- Intense "AI bubble" debate. Total AI spending projected to surpass $1.6T; OpenAI committed ~$1.4T over 8 years on data centers; the big four (Google, Microsoft, Meta, Amazon) expected to spend ~$450B in capex in 2026; the 14 largest data-center operators near $750B in 2026 (up from ~$450B in 2025); Goldman estimates ~$7.6T cumulative AI CapEx 2026–2031.
- Concentration risk: in late 2025 the five largest companies held up ~30% of the S&P 500 — the greatest concentration in half a century. AI-related stocks have driven ~75% of S&P 500 returns, 80% of earnings growth, and 90% of capex growth since ChatGPT launched.
- Circular-investment concerns (Nvidia↔OpenAI↔AMD↔Microsoft↔CoreWeave cross-holdings) fuel bubble skepticism. Hyperscalers took on ~$121B in new debt (+300% vs. typical load), some structured off-balance-sheet via special-purpose vehicles.
- Revenue/ROI doubts: an MIT study found 95% of organizations saw zero return on GenAI investment; one analysis found only ~3% of people pay for AI. A Feb 2026 NBER study found 90% of firms reported no productivity/workplace impact.
- Institutional warnings: the Bank of England and IMF have warned of global market-correction risk from AI overvaluation. Counter-view (JPMorgan, Fed Chair Powell, White House AI czar David Sacks, Ben Horowitz) holds AI generates real revenue and is not a classic bubble. DeepSeek's Jan 2025 release triggered a ~$600B single-day Nvidia drop; the KOSPI halted trading on June 23, 2026.

## Environmental
- Energy is becoming a binding constraint on AI buildout. The IEA projects global data-center electricity use rising from ~415 TWh in 2024 (~1.5% of total) toward ~945 TWh by 2030 (roughly 4×).
- Data-center siting, water use, and grid strain are emerging as local political flashpoints. Public opinion favors data-center approaches that protect consumers from high electricity costs and environmental impact.
- Grid capacity, power procurement, and cooling are now first-order bottlenecks alongside chip supply.

## Political
- The federal posture is deregulatory and pro-preemption. A Dec 11, 2025 executive order ("Ensuring a National Policy Framework for AI," EO 14365) directs a DOJ litigation task force (active Jan 10, 2026) to challenge state AI laws, a Commerce review of "burdensome" state laws, and an FTC policy stance; it exempts child safety, AI compute/data-center infrastructure, and state procurement from preemption.
- In March 2026 the White House released a National Policy Framework urging Congress to enact broad preemption under a light-touch standard.
- AI is politically "up for grabs" — voters trust neither party to handle it well; negative sentiment concentrates among younger voters and women under 50.
- A notable administration reversal: driven by national-security concerns over advanced model capabilities, the administration is reportedly considering pre-release frontier-model evaluation requirements, and CAISI (the renamed US AI Safety Institute) announced evaluation partnerships with Google, Microsoft, and xAI — a shift for an administration that entered opposing AI oversight.

## Legal / Regulatory
- Preemption is NOT settled law. A 10-year state-law moratorium failed in the Senate 99-1 (July 2025). State laws remain enforceable unless/until preempted by statute or struck down by courts. Prudent posture is to keep complying with state law while monitoring.
- California SB 53 (Transparency in Frontier AI Act) is in effect as of Jan 1, 2026: frontier developers (>10^26 FLOPS) must publish risk frameworks, report safety incidents, and provide whistleblower protections; penalties up to $1M/violation for firms over $500M revenue. Also effective: AB 2013 (training-data transparency); SB 942 (AI-content disclosure/watermarking, delayed to Aug 2, 2026).
- Texas TRAIGA (HB 149) in force Jan 1, 2026 — scaled back from an EU-style draft to prohibiting specific harmful practices, intent-based liability, AG enforcement with a 60-day cure period.
- Colorado's comprehensive SB 24-205 never took effect; in May 2026 it was repealed/replaced by the narrower SB 26-189 (ADMT-focused), effective Jan 1, 2027. It was the only state law named in the Dec 2025 EO.
- New York amended the RAISE Act (March 2026) to mirror SB 53 (revenue-based threshold), easing multistate compliance.
- A bipartisan federal discussion draft — the Great American AI Act (June 4, 2026, Obernolte/Trahan, 269 pages) — proposes the first comprehensive federal framework in exchange for a 3-year freeze on state laws regulating how models are *built* (not how they're *used*). Not yet introduced or voted on.
- State legislative volume is high: ~1,561 AI bills introduced across 45 states as of March 2026, surpassing all of 2024.
- EU AI Act: GPAI obligations took effect Aug 2, 2025; high-risk obligations have an Aug 2, 2026 deadline, though the Commission proposed (Nov 2025) delaying high-risk implementation toward 2027. It remains the binding global benchmark.

## Security / Geopolitical
- State-linked misuse of frontier AI is documented and rising. In June 2026, OpenAI disclosed PRC-linked influence operations using ChatGPT to amplify US domestic grievances — notably targeting the data-center/electricity-cost debate — rated low-impact (Category One) but notable for testing narratives against AI infrastructure itself.
- US–China competition centers on compute: export controls on advanced chips and high-bandwidth memory; Chinese stockpiling ahead of restrictions; Chinese model developers (DeepSeek and others) demonstrating competitive capability at lower cost.
- Frontier cyber capability is operationalizing: documented cases of AI-assisted/AI-orchestrated cyber operations and the first AI-developed exploit code; labs now rate some models "High" in cyber capability.
- National-security concern over advanced model capabilities is reshaping US oversight posture (see Political) — government model-review arrangements with major labs are now in place.
- Critical-infrastructure exposure (power grid, financial systems) to AI-enabled attacks is a growing institutional concern.

## Demographic
- Attitudes split sharply by group. Data for Progress (Feb 2026): men favorable +16, women unfavorable -10; under-45 favorable +25, 45+ unfavorable -10; Black voters +29 and Latino +10 favorable, white voters -3.
- Generational pessimism is strongest among the young despite high usage: Quinnipiac found Gen Z 36% support / 58% oppose AI, the most negative of any generation; NBC found 18-34s at net -44 favorability and women 18-49 at -41.
- A usage/education divergence at work: among employed college-educated voters, daily AI use jumped from 22% (Aug 2025) to 34% (early 2026), while non-college use fell ~6 points — a widening adoption gap.
- Overall adoption keeps rising: ~56-66% report using an AI tool in recent months; professional/managerial and white-collar workers adopt far faster than blue-collar workers and retirees.

# Current-State Brief — Ground Truth for Flagged Topics

*Topics flagged: Agentic Behavior Monitoring; Scalable Oversight. Standing SOCIETAL_CONTEXT last updated June 2026.*

**EU AI Act high-risk obligations — UPDATES context.** The standing file lists the Nov 2025 delay as merely a Commission *proposal*. It has since advanced: on May 7, 2026, the Council of the EU and the European Parliament announced their provisional agreement on targeted amendments to the EU AI Act, as part of the European Commission's Digital Omnibus initiative. High-risk obligations for stand-alone Annex III systems are deferred to 2 December 2027; for AI embedded in regulated products under Annex I, to 2 August 2028. Note: these changes only take legal effect upon formal adoption and publication in the Official Journal, expected before 2 August 2026. 2 August 2026 remains an active compliance date.

**CAISI evaluations — extends/confirms context.** The file noted Google/Microsoft/xAI partnerships. Update: on May 5, 2026, NIST's Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) announced pre-deployment testing agreements with Google DeepMind, Microsoft, and xAI, expanding its frontier model evaluation program to five major labs alongside existing partners OpenAI and Anthropic. Evaluations now number more than 40 completed assessments including unreleased models, covering cybersecurity, biosecurity, and chemical weapons risks.

**Great American AI Act — confirms context.** As of June 2026 it is not law: it is a 269-page bipartisan discussion draft released June 4, 2026, by Reps. Obernolte and Trahan, proposing a three-year freeze on state laws regulating how AI models are built.

**California SB 53 — confirms context.** Signed September 29, 2025; took effect January 1, 2026, directly relevant to oversight/incident-reporting duties.

**Agentic misuse evidence (new).** Anthropic examined 832 accounts banned for malicious cyber activity between March 2025 and March 2026, mapping them onto MITRE ATT&CK — bearing directly on agentic behavior monitoring.
